request.question: KOIL ran a radio show featuring Mark Levin who worked for which Attorney General?

title: George J. Terwilliger III
Mark Levin
WHLO
The Liberty Amendments
WNIX
Earth 2 (TV series)
KOIL
WWLZ
WTMA
The Mark Levin Show
sentences: George J. Terwilliger III (born June 5, 1950) is an American lawyer and politician.
He is a partner in the Washington, D.C. office of McGuireWoods LLP and is a former United States Deputy Attorney General and acting United States Attorney General.
Terwilliger, of Vermont, was nominated on February 14, 1992, by President George H.W. Bush to be Deputy Attorney General at the U.S. Department of Justice.
He would succeed William Pelham Barr.

As Deputy Attorney General, Terwilliger became the second-highest-ranking official in the United States Department of Justice (DOJ) and ran the day-to-day operations of the Department, serving in that position from 1991 through 1993.
He was appointed to the position after serving as the United States Attorney for the District of Vermont.
In addition, he currently serves on the Advisory Board of Intellaine, LLP, a U.S. defense and risk engineering firm located in Arlington, Virginia.
Mark Reed Levin ( ; born September 21, 1957) is an American lawyer, author, and radio personality.
He is the host of syndicated radio show "The Mark Levin Show".
Levin worked in the administration of President Ronald Reagan and was a chief of staff for Attorney General Edwin Meese.
He is president of the Landmark Legal Foundation, has authored seven books, and contributes commentary to various media outlets such as "National Review Online".
On September 1, 2015, Levin was named Editor-in-Chief of Conservative Review.
WHLO (640 AM) – branded 640 WHLO – is a commercial radio station licensed to Akron, Ohio, serving both the Akron and Canton metro areas.
Owned by iHeartMedia, the station broadcasts primarily a news/talk format.

WHLO is the local affiliate for Fox News Radio; conservative talk programs "The Rush Limbaugh Show", "The Sean Hannity Show", "The Mark Levin Show", and "The Schnitt Show"; and overnight night talk shows "The Dave Ramsey Show" and "Coast to Coast AM".
The station also serves as the radio home of the Kent State Golden Flashes.
The WHLO studios are located in North Canton, while the station transmitter resides in Copley.
In addition to a standard analog transmission, WHLO is available online via iHeartRadio.
The Liberty Amendments: Restoring the American Republic is a book by the American talk radio host and lawyer Mark Levin, published in 2013.
In it, Levin lays out and makes a case for eleven Constitutional amendments which he believes would restore the Constitution’s chief components: federalism, republicanism, and limited government.
WNIX (1330 AM) is a radio station broadcasting a News-Talk format, featuring Rush Limbaugh, Michael Savage, Mark Levin, Doug Stephan and Dave Ramsey.
Licensed to Greenville, Mississippi, USA, the station is currently owned by Delta Radio Network LLC.
Earth 2 is an American science fiction television series which aired on NBC from November 6, 1994 to June 4, 1995.
The show was canceled after one season of 22 episodes.

It follows the journey and settlement of a small expeditionary group called the Eden Project, with the intent to journey to an Earth-like planet called G889 in an attempt to find a cure to an illness called "the syndrome".

The series was created by Michael Duggan, Carol Flint, Mark Levin, and Billy Ray, produced by Amblin Entertainment and Universal Television, and filmed primarily in northern New Mexico around the Santa Fe area.
The series' music was composed by David Bergeaud, and the executive producers were Michael Duggan, Mark Levin, and Carol Flint.
KOIL (1290 kHz) is a commercial AM radio station, licensed to Omaha, Nebraska.
It is owned by NRG Media (headquartered in Cedar Rapids, Iowa) and airs a Talk radio format.
KOIL's weekday schedule is mostly nationally syndicated talk shows such as Don Imus, Laura Ingraham, Sean Hannity, Mark Levin, Jim Bohannon, Clyde Lewis and Jerry Doyle.
It also airs games from the Omaha Lancers junior ice hockey team.
KOIL's studios are located at Dodge Street and 50th Avenue in Midtown Omaha, and its transmitter site is located in Bellevue, Nebraska.
KOIL operates at 5000 watts around the clock but at night it uses a directional antenna to protect other stations on 1290 kHz.
WWLZ (820 AM) is a radio station in the Elmira-Corning market in New York state.

It is a talk radio station that airs the Premiere Networks lineup ("Glenn Beck Radio Program", "The Rush Limbaugh Show", "The Sean Hannity Show" and "Coast to Coast AM") during the workday and overnight, Cumulus Media Networks programs ("First Light", "Imus in the Morning", "The Mark Levin Show", "The John Batchelor Show") during the evening and in morning drive, MRN races and Elmira Jackals hockey.
WTMA (branded as News Talk 1250 WTMA) is an AM radio station licensed to Charleston, South Carolina with a Talk radio format.
This station operates on 1250 kHz and is under the ownership of Cumulus Media.
WTMA airs a local news and interview morning show hosted by Charlie James.
The rest of the weekday schedule is made up of nationally syndicated talk shows from Cumulus subsidiary Westwood One Networks.
They include Chris Plante, Clark Howard, Michael Savage, Mark Levin, John Batchelor, Red Eye Radio and First Light.
Weekends feature shows on money, health, real estate and gardening, with syndicated shows from Larry Kudlow, Bob Brinker and repeats of weekday shows.
Each hour begins with Westwood One News.
The Mark Levin Show is a conservative talk radio show hosted by Mark Levin.
The program is broadcast nationwide on Westwood One and reaches an estimated seven million weekly listeners, according to an estimate from "Talkers Magazine".

The "Talkers" estimate puts Levin's listenership in a tie with "The Glenn Beck Radio Program" for fourth most-listened-to talk radio show in the United States and, counting all radio formats, tied for ninth most-listened-to radio program in the United States.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

Levin worked in the administration of President Ronald Reagan and was a chief of staff for Attorney General Edwin Meese.  He is president of the Landmark Legal Foundation, has authored seven books, and contributes commentary to various media outlets such as "National Review Online".  On September 1, 2015, Levin was named Editor-in-Chief of Conservative Review.

KOIL | KOIL (1290 kHz) is a commercial AM radio station, licensed to Omaha, Nebraska.  It is owned by NRG Media (headquartered in Cedar Rapids, Iowa) and airs a Talk radio format.  KOIL's weekday schedule is mostly nationally syndicated talk shows such as Don Imus, Laura Ingraham, Sean Hannity, Mark Levin, Jim Bohannon, Clyde Lewis and Jerry Doyle.  It also airs games from the Omaha Lancers junior ice hockey team.  KOIL's studios are located at Dodge Street and 50th Avenue in Midtown Omaha, and its transmitter site is located in Bellevue, Nebraska.  KOIL operates at 5000 watts around the clock but at night it uses a directional antenna to protect other stations on 1290 kHz.

The Mark Levin Show | The Mark Levin Show is a conservative talk radio show hosted by Mark Levin.  The program is broadcast nationwide on Westwood One and reaches an estimated seven million weekly listeners, according to an estimate from "Talkers Magazine".  The "Talkers" estimate puts Levin's listenership in a tie with "The Glenn Beck Radio Program" for fourth most-listened-to talk radio show in the United States and, counting all radio formats, tied for ninth most-listened-to radio program in the United States.

Mark Levine (journalist) | Mark H. Levine (born May 7, 1966) is the Democratic Delegate to the Virginia House of Delegates from Virginia's 45th District, which encompasses the eastern half of the City of Alexandria, some of the northern West End, parts of South Arlington, and the Alexandria portion of Fairfax County.  A Constitutional lawyer, Levine served as Legislative Counsel to Congressman Barney Frank (D-MA) from 2001 to 2003 and was an early advocate in the "Marriage Equality” movement.  Since 2003, Levine has hosted a nationally syndicated progressive public policy radio program and worked as a TV pundit.  In 2014, Levine ran in the Democratic Primary for Virginia's 8th Congressional District last year, a seat currently held by Rep. Don Beyer.

WCHM | WCHM (1490 AM) and 96.7 FM is a radio station broadcasting a News Talk Information format.  Licensed to Clarkesville, Georgia, USA.  The station is currently owned by WCHM Radio, LLC, an affiliated company of Batten Communications, inc. and features programming from Premiere Radio Networks and FOX News Radio.  Major shows include Rush Limbaugh, Glenn Beck, Mark Levin, Sean Hannity, Clark Howard, Michael Savage and Coast to Coast with George Noory.  The station also features local programming and University of Georgia sports programming.

Ketzel Levine | Ketzel Levine is an American radio journalist who began her broadcast career in 1974.  She joined National Public Radio (NPR) in 1977 and worked, variously, as the network's arts producer, sports director, features reporter and garden expert.  From 2000 through 2008, she was senior correspondent for the NPR program "Morning Edition".  At the end of that year, due to cutbacks at the network, Levine was laid off, while working on a documentary series about Americans coping with economic stress and job loss.  Her final NPR broadcast was about how she, herself, had just lost her job.

WTMA | WTMA (branded as News Talk 1250 WTMA) is an AM radio station licensed to Charleston, South Carolina with a Talk radio format.  This station operates on 1250 kHz and is under the ownership of Cumulus Media.  WTMA airs a local news and interview morning show hosted by Charlie James.  The rest of the weekday schedule is made up of nationally syndicated talk shows from Cumulus subsidiary Westwood One Networks.  They include Chris Plante, Clark Howard, Michael Savage, Mark Levin, John Batchelor, Red Eye Radio and First Light.  Weekends feature shows on money, health, real estate and gardening, with syndicated shows from Larry Kudlow, Bob Brinker and repeats of weekday shows.  Each hour begins with Westwood One News.
response.summary_1: First-hop evidence: passage [1] states Mark Levin "was a chief of staff for Attorney General Edwin Meese." Bridge entity: Edwin Meese. Missing fact: none for identification—the passages already name the Attorney General Levin worked for; only supporting details (exact title wording or service dates) are not provided. Retrieval directions: 1) open Mark Levin's biography page (e.g., "Mark Levin" on Wikipedia or an official bio) to verify the chief-of-staff role and fetch exact title and dates; optionally 2) open the "Edwin Meese" page to confirm his term as Attorney General and Levin's role during that period. Clarification: none.
response.hop2_query:
1) Open the "Mark Levin" biography (e.g., "Mark Levin Wikipedia" or official bio). Search for lines stating Levin's role with the U.S. Attorney General and extract the name of the Attorney General (and service dates if listed).
2) (Optional) Open the "Edwin Meese" page ("Edwin Meese Wikipedia") to confirm his term as U.S. Attorney General and any mention of Mark Levin as his chief of staff.
hop2_docs: Mark Levin | Mark Reed Levin ( ; born September 21, 1957) is an American lawyer, author, and radio personality.  He is the host of syndicated radio show "The Mark Levin Show".  Levin worked in the administration of President Ronald Reagan and was a chief of staff for Attorney General Edwin Meese.  He is president of the Landmark Legal Foundation, has authored seven books, and contributes commentary to various media outlets such as "National Review Online".  On September 1, 2015, Levin was named Editor-in-Chief of Conservative Review.

Meese Report | The final report of the Attorney General's Commission on Pornography (usually referred to as (the) Meese Report, for U.S. Attorney General Edwin Meese) is the result of a comprehensive investigation into pornography ordered by U.S. President Ronald Reagan.  It was published in July 1986 and contains 1,960 pages.

Edwin Meese | Edwin Meese III (born December 2, 1931) is an American attorney, law professor, author and member of the Republican Party who served in official capacities within the Ronald Reagan Gubernatorial Administration (1967–1974), the Reagan Presidential Transition Team (1980) and the Reagan White House (1981–1985), eventually rising to hold the position of the 75th Attorney General of the United States (1985–1988).

Arnold Burns | Arnold Irwin Burns (April 14, 1930 – October 1, 2013) was an American lawyer.  He served as the United States Deputy Attorney General from 1986 to 1988 under President Ronald Reagan and U.S. Attorney General Edwin Meese.  In March 1988, Burns, together with the head of the U.S. Justice Department's criminal division William Weld and four aides, resigned from office in protest of what they viewed as improper conduct by Attorney General Meese, including personal financial indiscretions.  In July 1988, Burns and Weld jointly testified before the U.S. Congress in support of a potential prosecution of Meese following an investigation by a special prosecutor, who had declined to file charges.  Meese resigned from office later in July 1988, shortly after Burns and Weld appeared before Congress.
Daniel Levin (writer) | Daniel Levin is an American writer, producer, and attorney.  His first novel, published in 2009, made the "New York Times" bestseller list.

Tennessee Attorney General | The Tennessee Attorney General (officially, Attorney General and Reporter) is a position within the Tennessee state government.  The Attorney General is the chief law enforcement officer and lawyer for Tennessee.  Unlike any other state, the Tennessee Attorney General is an officer of the judicial branch, being appointed by the justices of the Tennessee Supreme Court, rather than the Governor, or being elected by popular vote or vote of the legislature, for a term of eight years.  The current office holder is Herbert Slatery, who was appointed by the Tennessee Supreme Court for an eight-year term in 2014 to fill that position.  His service officially began with his swearing in by Tennessee Governor Bill Haslam, a close personal friend whom he had previously served as legal counsel, on October 1, 2014.

The Mark Levin Show | The Mark Levin Show is a conservative talk radio show hosted by Mark Levin.  The program is broadcast nationwide on Westwood One and reaches an estimated seven million weekly listeners, according to an estimate from "Talkers Magazine".  The "Talkers" estimate puts Levin's listenership in a tie with "The Glenn Beck Radio Program" for fourth most-listened-to talk radio show in the United States and, counting all radio formats, tied for ninth most-listened-to radio program in the United States.
response.summary_2: Mark Levin served as chief of staff to U.S. Attorney General Edwin Meese. Edwin Meese was the 75th Attorney General of the United States, serving under President Reagan from 1985–1988, which places Levin's role in Meese's Justice Department tenure.
